reasoning: Confirmed real. Evidence: (1) Line 134 registers 'wp_ajax_bstw_visual_editor_disabled_dismiss_notice' pointing to visual_editor_disabled_dismiss_notice(). (2) Lines 508-511: the callback contains only `add_user_meta(get_current_user_id(), '_bstw_visual_editor_disabled_notice_dismissed', true)` with no check_ajax_referer(), wp_verify_nonce(), or current_user_can() call anywhere in the file (grep confirmed zero matches). (3) The JS at lines 350-356 of black-studio-tinymce-widget.js sends an AJAX POST with only `action: 'bstw_visual_editor_disabled_dismiss_notice'` and no nonce parameter (grep on 'nonce' also confirmed zero matches). Reachability is correct: the wp_ajax_ prefix (not wp_ajax_nopriv_) means WordPress enforces a valid logged-in session cookie, so only authenticated users can trigger it. CSRF is possible: any logged-in user visiting an attacker-controlled page can have their browser silently dismiss this notice via a cross-origin AJAX POST. Impact is low (permanently writes a single user-meta key suppressing a UI notice) but the vulnerability is present and the pattern is textbook CSRF on an authenticated AJAX handler.
